---
title: "1.6 Compound Assignment Operators"
subtitle: "Part 1: Compound Assignment and Increment Basics"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Shortcuts that update and assign

<span class="term">Compound assignment operators</span> (复合赋值运算符) `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, and `%=` are shortcuts that do a math operation and assignment in one step.

For example, `x += 1` adds `1` to the current value of `x` and assigns the result back to `x`.

It is the same as:

```java
x = x + 1;
```

## Operator Then Equal Sign

### Remember `+=`, not `=+`

If you need a mnemonic to remember whether the compound operators are written like `+=` or `=+`, remember that the operation (`+`) is done first to produce the new value.

The new value is then assigned (`=`) back to the variable.

So it is <span class="mark">operator then equal sign</span>: `+=`.

## Increment and Decrement

### Extra concise operators

Since changing the value of a variable by one is especially common, Java has two extra concise operators:

::: {.tight-list}
- `++`: plus-plus or <span class="term">increment</span> (递增) operator
- `--`: minus-minus or <span class="term">decrement</span> (递减) operator
:::

They set a variable to one greater or one less than its current value.

## Equivalent Updates

### Three ways to add one

`x++` is an even more concise way to write `x = x + 1` than the compound operator `x += 1`.

```java
x++;
x += 1;
x = x + 1;
```

Similarly, `y--` is a concise way to write:

```java
y = y - 1;
```

These shortcuts only exist for `+` and `-`.

## Naming Note

### Why C++ is called C++

If you've heard of the programming language `C++`, the name is an inside joke.

`C`, an earlier language which `C++` is based on, had been incremented or improved to create `C++`.

## Operator Table {.table-fit}

### Written out, compound, extra concise

| Operator | `+` | `-` | `*` | `/` | `%` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Written out | `x = x + 1` | `x = x - 1` | `x = x * 2` | `x = x / 2` | `x = x % 2` |
| Compound | `x += 1` | `x -= 1` | `x *= 2` | `x /= 2` | `x %= 2` |
| Extra concise | `x++` | `x--` |  |  |  |

Run the code in the next slides to see these shortcut operators in action.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: compound-ops`

Textbook prompt: Run the code below to see what the `++` and shortcut operators do.

Click on the Show Code Lens button in Runestone to trace through the code and the variable values change in the visualizer.

Create more compound assignment statements with shortcut operators as described below and work with a partner to guess what they would print out before running the code.

## Starter Code

### `activecode:: compound-ops`

```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int score = 0;
        System.out.println(score);
        score++;
        System.out.println(score);
        score *= 2;
        System.out.println(score);
        int penalty = 5;
        score -= penalty / 2;
        System.out.println(score);
        // 1. Write a compound statement that adds 3 to score

        // 2. Write a compound statement that divides score by 2
    }
}
```

## Expected Output

### Before adding the two new statements

Runestone expects the original program output to be:

```text
0
1
2
0
```

Trace the value of `score` after each update before adding new statements.

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: compound-ops`

Runestone checks these requirements:

::: {.tight-list}
- the original output from `main` is `0`, `1`, `2`, `0`
- the code contains `+=`
- the code contains `/=`
:::

The two added statements should use the compound assignment form, not a fully written-out assignment.

## Prefix and Postfix Note

### Legal Java, but not AP exam focus

In real-world Java code, you may occasionally see the `++` and `--` operators used before the variable name, like `++x` rather than `x++`.

That is legal but not something that you will see on the AP exam.

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">postfix</span> operator: the operator is after the variable name, as in `x++`
- <span class="term">prefix</span> operator: the operator is before the variable name, as in `++x`
:::

## Prefix and Postfix Example

### When the expression value matters

If `x` is `10`:

```java
System.out.println(x++);
```

it will print `10`, but afterwards `x` will be `11`.

If `x` is `10`:

```java
System.out.println(++x);
```

it will print `11`, and afterwards the value will be `11`.

The AP exam will never use the prefix form, nor will it use postfix operators in a context where the value of the expression matters.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, and `%=` combine arithmetic and assignment
- rewrite `x += 1` as `x = x + 1`
- identify `++` as increment and `--` as decrement
- trace the `score` output in `compound-ops`
- add the required `+=` and `/=` statements without replacing the starter code
- state that AP CSA does not use prefix `++x` or tricky postfix expression values
:::

## End

### Continue to tracing

The next deck will cover the Check Your Understanding questions and the Code Tracing Challenge.
